God says in verse number four of Psalm 81, this was a statute for Israel and a law of the God of Jacob. This he ordained, he, it was God who appointed or instituted this. I mean they were crying out to God, were they not? Now we understand after the deliverance there were times in which they cried out and they complained and they said, would to God we were back in Egypt, how soon they were to forget how bad it was in Egypt and how good it was that God had delivered them. But we find that as we look here that God says I removed his shoulder from the burden, so again we must acknowledge that it was God who did it. Now we know, we can go back to the account in the book of Exodus and we can read about the the Exodus and God's deliverance and we can understand with great clarity that God did it. And we can understand that the children of Israel, those that were there experiencing that first generation as they would go forth, they were eyewitnesses of God's great power, they knew that God did it. And you know it's interesting because we begin to look here and understand and we're reminded of not only God's directive that they were to observe this feast of tabernacles, that it was a statute in a law, that it was appointed by God to be a testimony or a witness to them, that it would be in that same time that as they followed God's directive they were at the very same time being reminded of God's deliverance. Isn't that pretty awesome? Because God's directives, God's commandments always have a purpose and they're always designed for us as the people of God to recognize the glory of God and to recognize what he has done and it brings us to that place of praise. We can learn from it but then we begin to think about some of the the directives that God has given to us as a local church. But then as we come to 1 Corinthians 11, we find that God has given us directive. He's given to us instruction, a command. He just simply says for as oft as you do. I'm simply saying tonight that as we look at the Old Testament truth, that we find that God has given to us some of the same types of things that he gave to Israel. And he gave these things to Israel, why? Because humans are prone to forget. It's not that they forgot that they came out of Egypt. It's not that that first generation forgot, or even generations down the road would have forgotten about their deliverance, but it doesn't maybe matter as much. It's just not on the forefront of their memory, and it's not something that really impacts them. And it's interesting because in 2 Peter, the Bible says in chapter one in verse number eight, for if these things be in you and abound, they make you that you shall neither be barren nor unfruitful in the knowledge of our Lord Jesus Christ. But he that lacketh these things is blind and cannot see afar off, and hath forgotten that he was purged from his old sins. Now, is it that he forgot, like he didn't know he was saved anymore? And so God gave to us the Lord's Supper, and we find that he told us that as often as we eat this bread and drink this cup, what do we do? You do show the Lord's death till he come. And so we find that the gospel is clearly given to us with regard to observing the Lord's Supper and understanding the fact that he, the Lord Jesus Christ, God in flesh, the just for the unjust, he's perfectly righteous, but his body was broken for us, that his precious blood was shed for us. There's nothing about the elements of the Lord's Supper that could ever save a person, but they certainly remind the saved person of what it cost for us to be saved. Amen. Often as we think about the latter chapters of the gospel records, in fact, I think, and I mentioned this several years ago, anytime you feel like you're kind of getting in a maybe a spiritual rut, you kind of feel like it's just kind of, things aren't going so well, just read the last couple of chapters of each of the gospels, because you'll be reminded of what Jesus did for you, how much he loves you. It's not a religious, I shouldn't use the word spiritual thing. It's not a rote religion. It's not just a mindless exercise, a dead orthodoxy type of a task and an approach. No, we're being reminded of what cost our Savior so that we could be redeemed. When I observe the Lord's Supper, I'm reminded that I'm saved because I accepted the Lord Jesus Christ as my personal Savior. The elements, again, they can't save you. We understand that. And it's different than the Feast of Tabernacles. And what was it that they're remembering? Their deliverance. Because he says there, as we looked in verse number six of Psalm 81, he makes it very plain that it was him, the Lord, that delivered. I'm thankful that they never went back into Egypt. And I'm thankful that for us this evening, that once we're saved, we can never lose our salvation, that we're secure in the hand of God. But could I encourage you this evening, we'll look at it a little bit more next Wednesday evening as we get into verse number seven. When you got saved and when I got saved, there was no promise that it was gonna be a perfect road with no problems. There's a great difference between a saved person and a lost person when it comes to problems. And that is that we have someone to go with us and someone that's able to deliver us. And we'll see that as we study into verse number seven next week. And for us this evening as we follow the directive that God has given to us, there's a purpose in all of the directives that God gives, of all of the commandments that God gives and He's causing us to be reminded, to be reminded. Some of you got saved out of a life of just all kinds of trouble and difficulty and some were still sinners and needed to be saved but never experienced that level of depravity that some others have. You heard the comments of the man that was preaching on Sunday with regard to some of what he dealt with in his life and what God saved him out of. Every one of us though needs to be remembered or be reminded that we've been saved. Listen, you don't have to have had a past that's in deep sin to be thankful for being saved. Because as it is written, there's none righteous, no not one. All have sinned and come short of the glory of God. And when you come to church, be reminded, this doing remembrance of me. See, God wanted the nation of Israel to be reminded of what He did for them. And I as a New Testament believer want to be reminded every day of what God did for me. I don't want to wait to just one time a year. I know we have Thanksgiving, it's November.
